summary: Gruber examines the Mac OS X Panther adoption rate among his readers, noting that about 10% held off for months after release. He uses Jeffrey Zeldman's disastrous Panther upgrade experience — which killed his PowerBook over three days — to illustrate why cautious users wait. While acknowledging that bugs are inevitable and Panther works fine for most people, Gruber argues that OS X has a troubling failure mode: when things go wrong, they go deeply wrong in ways that require Unix expertise to fix. He contends that Apple risks alienating the creative professionals who kept the company alive through its hardest years, and that OS X should never require command-line knowledge to recover from a software update.
key_points: About 90% of Gruber's readers had upgraded to Panther by April 2004, but the remaining holdouts were cautious professionals who couldn't risk downtime
Zeldman's Panther upgrade caused cascading failures — broken apps, kernel panics, and an unbootable machine — taking three days to recover
A post-upgrade printing bug caused by missing system users exemplifies how OS X's Unix underpinnings create failure modes that ordinary Mac users can't diagnose
Mac OS 9 failures were more recoverable by non-technical users; OS X failures happen at deeper system levels requiring Unix knowledge
Creative professionals who kept Apple profitable through hard times deserve an upgrade experience that never demands command-line intervention
money_quotes: The fact the Panther upgrade worked perfectly for you, and for most of us, is not proof that Mr. Zeldman must have done something wrong.
Bugs happen. Some will slip through even the tightest QA tests. It has always been the case, and always will be, that every upgrade of your OS ought to be preceded by a full backup.
I no longer remember trouble-shooting tips, I trust Google to remember them for me.
When things go wrong on Mac OS X, they often happen at a deeper level. File permission and ownership problems, for example, are not something a typical Mac user can deal with.
It's not enough that Mac OS X doesn't require any Unix nerdery whatsoever for day-to-day use — it should never require Unix nerdery to recover from software updates, either.
There is a huge 'it just works' advantage that Mac OS 9 holds over OS X, and Zeldman's recent experience exemplifies it.
It's also the case that design professionals tend to purchase Apple's higher-margin, top-of-the-line hardware. They're not just loyal Apple customers, they're profitable Apple customers.
key_insight: Mac OS X should never require Unix expertise to recover from a failed software update, because the creative professionals who depend on it — and who kept Apple alive — deserve better than that.
